query: Vue ref vs reactive interview question

competitors.label: Reactivity API: Core
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue API reference for ref, reactive, shallow variants, and core reactivity behavior.
theirStrengths: The official API reference is authoritative on what ref and reactive return and how the APIs behave.
theirGaps: It is less focused on the practical trap patterns developers hit when destructuring or replacing reactive objects.

realWorldUseCases: Most bugs come from destructuring , replacing reactive objects , and losing reactivity by pulling values out of proxies .
actionableExamples: Fix: use toRefs() (or toRef() ) so destructured fields stay reactive.

realWorldUseCases: The API docs define ref and reactive precisely, but they are less centered on the 'why isn't this updating?' debugging scenarios.
actionableExamples: They are authoritative on the API, yet our page gives a more direct trap-and-fix walkthrough.
followUpConfusion: The official docs still go deeper on the full API surface and shallow variants, so the follow-up criterion remains close.
Add one small note on shallowRef or shallowReactive so the API-surface follow-up is less lopsided.

competitors.winReason: The API reference is canonical, but our page is more directly useful for debugging because it centers the destructuring and replacement traps developers actually hit.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because reactivity and Composition API choices are core Vue interview topics.
theirStrengths: It is a strong broad Vue interview-prep hub with reactivity and Composition API coverage.
theirGaps: The landing page is not a direct ref-versus-reactive explainer and is lighter on concrete trap patterns.

realWorldUseCases: This is the #1 “why isn’t my UI updating?” moment.
followUpConfusion: use ref for single values (especially primitives) and for values you’ll replace ; use reactive for “state objects” you mostly mutate .

realWorldUseCases: The bank is directly relevant at the Vue fundamentals level, but it is less focused on this exact destructuring or replacement bug.
actionableExamples: Because GreatFrontEnd emphasizes solved examples, the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about choosing ref for replaceable values and reactive for mutable state objects.

actionableExamples: Keep one compact replace-whole-object example so the ref choice stays grounded.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is a strong broad Vue interview resource, but our page is more specific on the practical decision rule and the debugging traps around destructuring and replacement.

competitors.label: Vue 3 Composition API: ref() and reactive()
competitors.selectionReason: ThisDot has a direct topic article comparing ref and reactive in Vue 3.
theirStrengths: It is a direct topic article that explains the mechanics of both APIs clearly.
theirGaps: It is less focused on the two highest-friction trap patterns: destructuring and replacing a reactive object.

realWorldUseCases: Trap #2: replacing a reactive object breaks consumers
followUpConfusion: If you reassign the variable, anything holding the old proxy won’t see the new object.

realWorldUseCases: The article explains the API differences directly, but it is less centered on the exact stale-proxy and destructuring failure modes.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong direct comparator with practical examples, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about why replacing a reactive proxy breaks consumers and why toRefs preserves destructured reactivity.

actionableExamples: Add one smaller primitive-versus-object decision example so the page stays balanced and not only trap-focused.

competitors.winReason: ThisDot is a strong direct comparison article, but our page is more useful for follow-up debugging because it makes the destructuring and stale-proxy traps explicit.
